title: Using Categorical Covariates with AddiVortes
text: categorical variable d distinct levels treated number natural ordering magnitude categories. example, assigning “North” = 1, “South” = 2, “East” = 3, “West” = 4 incorrectly imply “West” four times “North”. One-hot encoding converts categorical variable d levels d − 1 binary (0/1) indicator columns. One level chosen reference level (convention, first level alphabetical order), remaining d − 1 levels receive column: reference level (“East” , alphabetically first) represented zeros. Using d − 1 rather d columns avoids perfect collinearity retaining full information group membership. AddiVortes applies encoding automatically column x type character factor. need pre-process data.

title: Using Categorical Covariates with AddiVortes
text: one-hot encoding, indicator column takes values 0 1, continuous covariates normalised range [−0.5, 0.5]. catScaling = 1 (default), binary jump 0 1 magnitude comparable full range normalised continuous covariate, giving categorical continuous covariates roughly equal influence Voronoi tessellation distances. can adjust catScaling argument: catScaling > 1: gives categorical differences weight continuous differences. catScaling < 1: gives categorical differences less weight, making model rely heavily continuous covariates. column name binary indicator follows pattern <original_column>_<level>. example, column region levels "East", "North", "South", "West" produces columns region_North, region_South, region_West ("East" reference).

title: Using Categorical Covariates with AddiVortes
text: create dataset 400 observations two continuous covariates two categorical covariates. response variable depends four: Note region 4 levels product 3 levels. passed AddiVortes character columns, encoded 3 2 binary columns respectively — total 5 extra columns alongside 2 continuous covariates.
code: library(AddiVortes)  set.seed(123) n <- 400  x <- data.frame(   age = rnorm(n, mean = 40, sd = 10),   income = runif(n, 20, 120), # income in thousands   region = sample(c("East", "North", "South", "West"), n, replace = TRUE),   product = sample(c("Basic", "Premium", "Deluxe"), n, replace = TRUE),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # True response: depends on continuous and categorical variables region_effect <- ifelse(x$region == "North", 5,   ifelse(x$region == "South", -5, 0) ) product_effect <- ifelse(x$product == "Premium", 10,   ifelse(x$product == "Deluxe", 20, 0) )  y <- 0.3 * x$age +   0.1 * x$income +   region_effect +   product_effect +   rnorm(n, sd = 3)

title: Using Categorical Covariates with AddiVortes
text: can call internal encoding function directly see exactly encoded matrix looks like fitting model. columns produced : - age income (unchanged continuous columns) - region_North, region_South, region_West (3 indicators; “East” reference) - product_Deluxe, product_Premium (2 indicators; “Basic” reference) binary columns take values 0 catScaling (1). catScaling = 1 indicator columns continuous columns span comparable range inside model.
code: # Show the first few rows of x before encoding head(x, 5) #>        age   income region product #> 1 34.39524 67.06818   East Premium #> 2 37.69823 56.58455   West   Basic #> 3 55.58708 32.12721  North Premium #> 4 40.70508 24.69937   East Premium #> 5 41.29288 46.27963   East   Basic # Manually inspect the encoding applied by AddiVortes enc_result <- AddiVortes:::encodeCategories_internal(x, catScaling = 1) head(enc_result$encoded, 5) #>           age   income region_North region_South region_West product_Deluxe #> [1,] 34.39524 67.06818            0            0           0              0 #> [2,] 37.69823 56.58455            0            0           1              0 #> [3,] 55.58708 32.12721            1            0           0              0 #> [4,] 40.70508 24.69937            0            0           0              0 #> [5,] 41.29288 46.27963            0            0           0              0 #>      product_Premium #> [1,]               1 #> [2,]               0 #> [3,]               1 #> [4,]               1 #> [5,]               0

title: Using Categorical Covariates with AddiVortes
text: Fitting model identical standard workflow — simply pass data frame character factor columns directly. AddiVortes handles encoding internally. encoding metadata stored fit$catEncoding automatically used making predictions, new data passed predict() encoded exactly reference levels.
code:
# Split into training and test sets set.seed(42) train_idx <- sample(n, 300)  x_train <- x[train_idx, ] y_train <- y[train_idx] x_test <- x[-train_idx, ] y_test <- y[-train_idx]  fit <- AddiVortes(   y = y_train,   x = x_train,   m = 50,   totalMCMCIter = 500,   mcmcBurnIn = 100,   catScaling = 1, # default: binary columns span [0, 1]   showProgress = FALSE ) cat("In-sample RMSE:", round(fit$inSampleRmse, 3), "
") #> In-sample RMSE: 2.393  # The catEncoding field records how the encoding was built cat("
Reference levels used:
") #>  #> Reference levels used: for (j in fit$catEncoding$catColIndices) {   orig_col <- fit$catEncoding$origColNames[j]   ref_lev <- fit$catEncoding$colEncodings[[j]]$levels[1]   all_lev <- fit$catEncoding$colEncodings[[j]]$levels   cat(     " ", orig_col, ": reference =", ref_lev,     "| all levels:", paste(all_lev, collapse = ", "), "
"   ) } #>   region : reference = East | all levels: East, North, South, West  #>   product : reference = Basic | all levels: Basic, Deluxe, Premium

title: Using Categorical Covariates with AddiVortes
text: Predictions new data work usual way. new data contains categorical levels training data, encoding applied consistently.
code: preds <- predict(fit, x_test, showProgress = FALSE) rmse_test <- sqrt(mean((y_test - preds)^2)) cat("Test RMSE:", round(rmse_test, 3), "\n") #> Test RMSE: 2.883 # Colour observations by product category prod_cols <- c("Basic" = "steelblue", "Premium" = "darkorange", "Deluxe" = "darkgreen") point_cols <- prod_cols[x_test$product]  plot(y_test, preds,   col = point_cols, pch = 19, cex = 0.8,   xlab = "Observed values",   ylab = "Predicted values",   main = "Predicted vs. Observed (coloured by product category)" ) abline(0, 1, lwd = 2, lty = 2, col = "grey40") legend("topleft",   legend = names(prod_cols),   col = prod_cols,   pch = 19, title = "Product", bty = "n" )

title: Using Categorical Covariates with AddiVortes
text: prediction time, new observation contains category level seen training, AddiVortes treats reference level (binary indicators set zero). sensible default: model infer anything previously unseen level falls back baseline.
code: # Create a test point with an unseen product level "Luxury" x_new <- data.frame(   age = 45,   income = 80,   region = "North",   product = "Luxury", # unseen level   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) pred_new <- predict(fit, x_new, showProgress = FALSE) cat(   "Prediction for unseen category 'Luxury' (treated as 'Basic'):",   round(pred_new, 3), "\n" ) #> Prediction for unseen category 'Luxury' (treated as 'Basic'): 25.783

title: Using Categorical Covariates with AddiVortes
text: catScaling parameter controls much influence categorical differences distance calculations. fit two models — one catScaling = 1 (equal weight) one catScaling = 2 (double weight categorical differences) — compare test RMSEs. example, true response substantial category effects (±20 units product type) relative continuous effects, increasing catScaling may help model focus categorical group membership.
code: fit_cs2 <- AddiVortes(   y = y_train,   x = x_train,   m = 50,   totalMCMCIter = 500,   mcmcBurnIn = 100,   catScaling = 2, # give categorical differences twice as much weight   showProgress = FALSE ) preds_cs2 <- predict(fit_cs2, x_test, showProgress = FALSE) cat("Test RMSE (catScaling = 1):", round(rmse_test, 3), "\n") #> Test RMSE (catScaling = 1): 2.883 cat("Test RMSE (catScaling = 2):", round(sqrt(mean((y_test - preds_cs2)^2)), 3), "\n") #> Test RMSE (catScaling = 2): 2.935

title: Using Categorical Covariates with AddiVortes
text: Pass character factor columns directly covariate data frame; AddiVortes encodes automatically. One-hot encoding: categorical variable d levels converted d − 1 binary (0/1) indicator columns. reference level alphabetically first level; indicators zero. Indicator columns named <original_column>_<level> (e.g. product_Premium). catScaling (default 1) controls weight given categorical differences relative continuous differences. Increase give categories influence; decrease give less. encoding metadata stored fit$catEncoding applied automatically new data predict(). Unseen category levels prediction time treated reference level.

title: Machine Learning with AddiVortes: A Bayesian Alternative to BART
text: First, load AddiVortes package. example, use well-known Boston Housing dataset, included within package Boston.
code: # Load the package require(AddiVortes)  # Separate predictors (X) and the response variable (Y) X_Boston <- as.matrix(Boston[, 1:13]) Y_Boston <- as.numeric(as.matrix(Boston[, 14]))

title: Machine Learning with AddiVortes: A Bayesian Alternative to BART
text: evaluate model’s performance, need split data training set testing set. use standard 5/6 split training 1/6 testing.
code: n <- length(Y_Boston)  # Set a seed for reproducibility set.seed(1025)  # Create a training set containing 5/6 of the data TrainSet <- sort(sample.int(n, 5 * n / 6))  # The remaining data will be our test set TestSet <- setdiff(1:n, TrainSet)

title: Machine Learning with AddiVortes: A Bayesian Alternative to BART
text: Now can run main AddiVortes function training data. specify several parameters algorithm, number iterations trees.
code: # Run the AddiVortes algorithm on the training data results <- AddiVortes(   y = Y_Boston[TrainSet],   x = X_Boston[TrainSet, ],   m = 200,   totalMCMCIter = 2000,   mcmcBurnIn = 200,   nu = 6,   q = 0.85,   k = 3,   sd = 0.8,   Omega = 3,   LambdaRate = 25,   InitialSigma = "Linear",   showProgress = FALSE )

title: Machine Learning with AddiVortes: A Bayesian Alternative to BART
text: trained model object, can now make predictions unseen test data. calculate Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) see well model performed.
code: # Generate predictions on the test set preds <- predict(results,   X_Boston[TestSet, ],   showProgress = FALSE )  # The RMSE is contained in the results object cat("In-Sample RMSE:", results$inSampleRmse, "\n") #> In-Sample RMSE: 1.182148  # Calculate the Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) for the test set rmse <- sqrt(mean((Y_Boston[TestSet] - preds)^2)) cat("Test Set RMSE:", rmse, "\n") #> Test Set RMSE: 3.206674

title: Machine Learning with AddiVortes: A Bayesian Alternative to BART
text: Finally, good way assess model plot predicted values true values. perfect model, points lie equality line (y = x). also plot prediction intervals visualise model’s uncertainty.
code: # Plot true values vs. predicted values plot(Y_Boston[TestSet],   preds,   xlab = "True Values",   ylab = "Predicted Values",   main = "AddiVortes Predictions vs True Values",   xlim = range(c(Y_Boston[TestSet], preds)),   ylim = range(c(Y_Boston[TestSet], preds)),   pch = 19, col = "darkblue" )  # Add the line of equality (y = x) for reference abline(a = 0, b = 1, col = "darkred", lwd = 2)  # Get quantile predictions to create error bars/intervals preds_quantile <- predict(results,   X_Boston[TestSet, ],   "quantile",   showProgress = FALSE )  # Add error segments for each prediction for (i in seq_len(nrow(preds_quantile))) {   segments(Y_Boston[TestSet][i], preds_quantile[i, 1],     Y_Boston[TestSet][i], preds_quantile[i, 2],     col = "darkblue", lwd = 1   ) } legend("topleft",   legend = c("Prediction", "y=x Line", "95% Interval"),   col = c("darkblue", "darkred", "darkblue"),   lty = 1, pch = c(19, NA, NA), lwd = 2 )

title: Bayesian Regression and Prediction with AddiVortes
text: start creating 5-dimensional dataset. response variable Y determined simple rule based first two predictors, X[,1] X[,2], plus random noise. allows us know “true” data-generating process, useful evaluating model.
code: # Load the package library(AddiVortes)  # --- Generate Training Data --- set.seed(42) # for reproducibility  # Create a 5-column matrix of predictors X <- matrix(runif(2500), ncol = 5) X[, 1] <- -10 - X[, 1] * 10 X[, 2] <- X[, 2] * 100 X[, 3] <- -9 + X[, 3] * 10 X[, 4] <- 8 + X[, 4] X[, 5] <- X[, 5] * 10  # Create the response 'Y' based on a rule and add noise Y_underlying <- ifelse(-1 * X[, 2] > 10 * X[, 1] + 100, 10, 0) Y <- Y_underlying + rnorm(length(Y_underlying))  # Visualise the relationships in the data # The colours show the two underlying groups pairs(X,   col = ifelse(Y_underlying == 10, "red", "blue"),   pch = 19, cex = 0.5,   main = "Structure of Predictor Variables" )

title: Bayesian Regression and Prediction with AddiVortes
text: Next, fit AddiVortes model training data. example, ’ll use small number trees (m=50) quick demonstration. -sample RMSE gives us idea well model fits data trained . However, true test model performance new data.
code: # Fit the model AModel <- AddiVortes(Y, X, m = 50, showProgress = FALSE) # We can check the in-sample Root Mean Squared Error cat("In-sample RMSE:", AModel$inSampleRmse, "\n") #> In-sample RMSE: 0.8454648

title: Bayesian Regression and Prediction with AddiVortes
text: evaluate model’s predictive power, generate new “--sample” test set using process . use predict() method fitted model (AModel) get predictions new data.
code: # --- Generate Test Data --- set.seed(101) # Use a different seed for the test set testX <- matrix(runif(1000), ncol = 5) testX[, 1] <- -10 - testX[, 1] * 10 testX[, 2] <- testX[, 2] * 100 testX[, 3] <- -9 + testX[, 3] * 10 testX[, 4] <- 8 + testX[, 4] testX[, 5] <- testX[, 5] * 10  # Create the true test response values testY_underlying <- ifelse(-1 * testX[, 2] > 10 * testX[, 1] + 100, 10, 0) testY <- testY_underlying + rnorm(length(testY_underlying))  # --- Make Predictions --- # Predict the mean response preds <- predict(AModel, testX,   showProgress = FALSE )  # Predict the 90% credible interval (from 0.05 to 0.95 quantiles) # By default, this uses interval = "credible" which only accounts for # uncertainty in the mean function preds_q <- predict(AModel, testX,   "quantile", c(0.05, 0.95),   showProgress = FALSE )  # For prediction intervals that also include the model's error variance # (similar to lm's prediction intervals), use interval = "prediction" # preds_q_pred <- predict(AModel, testX, #                         "quantile", c(0.05, 0.95), #                         interval = "prediction", #                         showProgress = FALSE)

title: Bayesian Regression and Prediction with AddiVortes
text: plot effective way visualise model’s performance. plot observed values test set model’s predicted mean values. red lines plot represent true, underlying mean values (0 10) data-generating process. good model produce predictions cluster around lines. blue vertical segments represent 90% credible intervals prediction, giving us sense model’s uncertainty.  plot shows model successfully learned underlying structure data predictions clustering correctly around true mean values 0 10.
code: # Plot observed vs. predicted values plot(testY,   preds,   xlab = "Observed Values",   ylab = "Predicted Mean Values",   main = "Out-of-Sample Prediction Performance",   xlim = range(c(testY, preds_q)),   ylim = range(c(testY, preds_q)),   pch = 19, col = "darkblue" )  # Add error lines for the 90% credible interval for (i in seq_len(nrow(preds_q))) {   segments(testY[i], preds_q[i, 1],     testY[i], preds_q[i, 2],     col = rgb(0, 0, 0.5, 0.5), lwd = 1.5   ) }  # Add lines showing the true underlying means lines(c(min(testY) - 0.2, 3), c(0, 0), col = "pink", lwd = 3, lty = 2) lines(c(7, max(testY) + 0.2), c(10, 10), col = "pink", lwd = 3, lty = 2)  # Add a legend legend("bottomright",   legend = c(     "Predicted Mean & 90% Interval",     "True Underlying Mean"   ),   col = c("darkblue", "pink"),   pch = c(19, NA),   lty = c(1, 2),   lwd = c(1.5, 3),   bty = "n" )

title: Bayesian Regression and Prediction with AddiVortes
text: predict() method supports two types intervals, similar R’s built-lm function: Credible intervals (interval = "credible", default): reflect uncertainty mean function E[Y|X]. account posterior sampling variability uncertainty tessellation structure, include inherent noise individual observations. Prediction intervals (interval = "prediction"): include uncertainty mean function model’s error variance (σ²). makes appropriate predicting individual future observations. Let’s compare two types intervals subset test data:  can see plot, prediction intervals (red) wider credible intervals (blue). prediction intervals account : Epistemic uncertainty: uncertainty true mean function (captured credible interval) Aleatoric uncertainty: inherent random noise individual observations (additional width prediction intervals) can quantify difference: use type: Use credible intervals want understand uncertainty mean response given set predictor values (e.g., “average outcome type case?”) Use prediction intervals want predict individual future observations (e.g., “range outcomes expect specific new case?”) distinction standard regression lm, AddiVortes additionally captures complex non-linear relationships tessellation-based approach.
code:
# Use a subset of test data for clearer visualization subset_indices <- 1:20 testX_subset <- testX[subset_indices, ] testY_subset <- testY[subset_indices]  # Get credible intervals (uncertainty in mean only) cred_intervals <- predict(AModel, testX_subset,   type = "quantile",   interval = "credible",   quantiles = c(0.025, 0.975),   showProgress = FALSE )  # Get prediction intervals (includes error variance) pred_intervals <- predict(AModel, testX_subset,   type = "quantile",   interval = "prediction",   quantiles = c(0.025, 0.975),   showProgress = FALSE )  # Get mean predictions mean_preds <- predict(AModel, testX_subset,   type = "response",   showProgress = FALSE )  # Create comparison plot plot(seq_along(testY_subset), testY_subset,   xlab = "Observation Index",   ylab = "Response Value",   main = "Credible Intervals vs. Prediction Intervals",   pch = 19, col = "black", cex = 1.2, ylim = c(-5, 20) )  # Add mean predictions points(seq_along(testY_subset), mean_preds, pch = 4, col = "blue", cex = 1)  # Add credible intervals (narrower, in blue) for (i in seq_along(testY_subset)) {   segments(i, cred_intervals[i, 1],     i, cred_intervals[i, 2],     col = "blue", lwd = 2   ) }  # Add prediction intervals (wider, in red) for (i in seq_along(testY_subset)) {   segments(i - 0.1, pred_intervals[i, 1],     i - 0.1, pred_intervals[i, 2],     col = "red", lwd = 2, lty = 1   ) }  # Add legend legend("topright",   legend = c(     "Observed Values", "Mean Prediction",     "95% Credible Interval", "95% Prediction Interval"   ),   col = c("black", "blue", "blue", "red"),   pch = c(19, 4, NA, NA),   lty = c(NA, NA, 1, 1),   lwd = c(NA, NA, 2, 2),   bty = "n" ) # Calculate average interval widths cred_width <- mean(cred_intervals[, 2] - cred_intervals[, 1]) pred_width <- mean(pred_intervals[, 2] - pred_intervals[, 1])  cat("Average 95% credible interval width:", round(cred_width, 2), "
") #> Average 95% credible interval width: 3.07 cat("Average 95% prediction interval width:", round(pred_width, 2), "
") #> Average 95% prediction interval width: 5.3 cat("Ratio (prediction/credible):", round(pred_width / cred_width, 2), "
") #> Ratio (prediction/credible): 1.72

title: Modelling Spherical Data with AddiVortes
text: Spherical data observations whose locations lie surface sphere. familiar example geographic data specified latitude longitude. Two important properties make different ordinary planar data: Wrap-around: Longitude values near −180° +180° actually close globe, Euclidean distance large. good distance metric must recognise . Curvature: Distances follow great-circle arcs (shortest path along sphere’s surface) rather straight lines. AddiVortes uses great-circle distance (also known geodesic distance haversine-type formula) metric = "S" specified. ensures points near poles near date line treated neighbours genuinely close sphere.

title: Modelling Spherical Data with AddiVortes
text: AddiVortes expects spherical coordinates radians, following convention: Latitude-type dimensions (spherical dimensions except last): values [−π/2, π/2]. Longitude / azimuthal dimension (last spherical column): values [−π, π]. single-surface model (globe), need exactly two columns: metric = "S". model use spherical (great-circle) distance pairs tessellation centres observations. data combine spherical non-spherical covariates, can pass vector metric indicators, e.g. metric = c(1, 1, 0) two spherical dimensions followed one Euclidean dimension.
code: x <- cbind(latitude_rad, longitude_rad)

title: Modelling Spherical Data with AddiVortes
text: simulate dataset 300 observations distributed across surface globe. observation latitude longitude, response variable represents smoothly varying quantity — , synthetic “temperature” warmest equator varies longitude. plot shows spatial distribution simulated response values:
code: library(AddiVortes)  set.seed(42) n <- 300  # Sample random locations on the globe lat <- runif(n, -pi / 2, pi / 2) # latitude in radians: [-pi/2, pi/2] lon <- runif(n, -pi, pi) # longitude in radians: [-pi, pi]  # True function: warmer at the equator, slight east-west gradient y_true <- 20 * cos(lat) + 5 * sin(lon)  # Add observation noise y <- y_true + rnorm(n, sd = 2)  # Covariate matrix: latitude first, longitude last (required convention) x <- cbind(lat, lon) # Colour scale for the response cols <- colorRampPalette(c("blue", "white", "red"))(100) col_index <- cut(y, breaks = 100, labels = FALSE)  # Convert radians to degrees for a readable plot lat_deg <- lat * 180 / pi lon_deg <- lon * 180 / pi  plot(lon_deg, lat_deg,   col = cols[col_index], pch = 19, cex = 0.7,   xlab = "Longitude (degrees)",   ylab = "Latitude (degrees)",   main = "Simulated Response on the Globe" ) legend("bottomleft",   legend = c("High", "Low"),   col = c("red", "blue"),   pch = 19, bty = "n" )

title: Modelling Spherical Data with AddiVortes
text: fit AddiVortes metric = "S" tell model columns spherical coordinates. model use great-circle distance assigning observations Voronoi cells. comparison, fit identical model using default Euclidean metric. model treats latitude longitude ordinary continuous variables handle wrap-around near ±π correctly.
code: fit_sph <- AddiVortes(   y = y,   x = x,   m = 50,   totalMCMCIter = 500,   mcmcBurnIn = 100,   metric = "S", # use great-circle distance for all columns   showProgress = FALSE ) cat("In-sample RMSE (spherical metric):", round(fit_sph$inSampleRmse, 3), "\n") #> In-sample RMSE (spherical metric): 1.284 fit_euc <- AddiVortes(   y = y,   x = x,   m = 50,   totalMCMCIter = 500,   mcmcBurnIn = 100,   metric = "E", # Euclidean distance (default)   showProgress = FALSE ) cat("In-sample RMSE (Euclidean metric):", round(fit_euc$inSampleRmse, 3), "\n") #> In-sample RMSE (Euclidean metric): 1.377

title: Modelling Spherical Data with AddiVortes
text: generate held-test set compare two models. also include group observations near date line (longitude ≈ ±π) highlight Euclidean metric likely struggle.
code: set.seed(101) n_test <- 200  lat_test <- runif(n_test, -pi / 2, pi / 2) lon_test <- runif(n_test, -pi, pi)  y_true_test <- 20 * cos(lat_test) + 5 * sin(lon_test) y_test <- y_true_test + rnorm(n_test, sd = 2)  x_test <- cbind(lat_test, lon_test) preds_sph <- predict(fit_sph, x_test, showProgress = FALSE) preds_euc <- predict(fit_euc, x_test, showProgress = FALSE) rmse_sph <- sqrt(mean((y_test - preds_sph)^2)) rmse_euc <- sqrt(mean((y_test - preds_euc)^2))  cat("Test RMSE — spherical metric:", round(rmse_sph, 3), "\n") #> Test RMSE — spherical metric: 5.536 cat("Test RMSE — Euclidean metric:", round(rmse_euc, 3), "\n") #> Test RMSE — Euclidean metric: 4.82

title: Modelling Spherical Data with AddiVortes
text: plot shows predicted versus observed values models test set. well-calibrated model points lying close diagonal.
code: y_range <- range(c(y_test, preds_sph, preds_euc))  plot(y_test, preds_sph,   pch = 19, col = "darkblue", cex = 0.7,   xlab = "Observed values",   ylab = "Predicted values",   main = "Spherical vs. Euclidean Metric: Predicted vs. Observed",   xlim = y_range, ylim = y_range ) points(y_test, preds_euc, pch = 4, col = "darkred", cex = 0.7) abline(0, 1, lwd = 2, lty = 2, col = "grey40") legend("topleft",   legend = c("Spherical metric", "Euclidean metric", "y = x"),   col    = c("darkblue", "darkred", "grey40"),   pch    = c(19, 4, NA),   lty    = c(NA, NA, 2),   lwd    = c(NA, NA, 2),   bty    = "n" )

title: Modelling Spherical Data with AddiVortes
text: Set metric = "S" (metric = "Spherical") use great-circle distance covariate columns. datasets mix spherical non-spherical covariates, pass vector 0s 1s, e.g. metric = c(1, 1, 0) two spherical dimensions one Euclidean dimension. Spherical coordinates must radians: latitude [−π/2, π/2] longitude [−π, π]. last spherical column treated azimuthal (longitude) dimension; spherical columns treated polar (latitude) dimensions. great-circle distance correctly handles wrap-around, points near longitude ±π recognised neighbours.

title: Authors and Citation

title: AddiVortes: Bayesian Machine Learning Alternative to BART
text: AddiVortes implements Bayesian Additive Voronoi Tessellation model machine learning regression non-parametric statistical modeling. R package provides flexible alternative BART (Bayesian Additive Regression Trees), using Voronoi tessellations instead trees spatial partitioning.

title: AddiVortes: Bayesian Machine Learning Alternative to BART
text: Machine Learning Regression: Advanced Bayesian regression modeling complex datasets Alternative BART: Uses Voronoi tessellations instead trees flexible spatial modeling Spatial Data Analysis: Excellent geographic spatial datasets Non-parametric Modeling: assumptions functional form Bayesian Framework: Full posterior inference uncertainty quantification Complex Function Approximation: Captures non-linear relationships interactions

title: AddiVortes: Bayesian Machine Learning Alternative to BART
text: AddiVortes particularly well-suited : Spatial regression geographic data analysis Machine learning tasks requiring interpretable models Non-parametric regression functional form unknown Bayesian modeling uncertainty quantification Complex surface modeling function approximation Alternative BART researchers seeking different ensemble approaches

title: AddiVortes: Bayesian Machine Learning Alternative to BART
text: can install latest version AddiVortes GitHub :
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("johnpaulgosling/AddiVortes",                           build_vignettes = TRUE)

title: AddiVortes: Bayesian Machine Learning Alternative to BART
code: library(AddiVortes)  # Load your data # X <- your_predictors # y <- your_response  # Fit the AddiVortes model # model <- AddiVortes(X, y)  # Make predictions # predictions <- predict(model, newdata = X_test)

title: AddiVortes: Bayesian Machine Learning Alternative to BART
text: Getting Started Guide Prediction Examples Function Reference

title: AddiVortes: Bayesian Machine Learning Alternative to BART
text: BART (Bayesian Additive Regression Trees) uses tree-based partitioning, AddiVortes uses Voronoi tessellations, can provide: natural spatial partitioning Flexible geometric boundaries Alternative ensemble approach machine learning Enhanced performance spatial data

title: AddiVortes: Bayesian Machine Learning Alternative to BART
text: use package research, please cite:
code: citation("AddiVortes")

title: AddiVortes: Bayesian Machine Learning Alternative to BART
text: Bayesian machine learning, BART alternative, Voronoi tessellation, spatial regression, non-parametric regression, ensemble methods, statistical modeling, R package

what: AddiVortes: Bayesian Additive Voronoi Tessellations for Machine Learning — AddiVortes-package
title: AddiVortes: Bayesian Additive Voronoi Tessellations for Machine Learning — AddiVortes-package
text: AddiVortes implements Bayesian Additive Voronoi Tessellation models machine learning regression non-parametric statistical modeling. package provides flexible alternative BART (Bayesian Additive Regression Trees), using Voronoi tessellations instead trees spatial partitioning. method particularly effective spatial data analysis, complex function approximation, Bayesian regression modeling.

title: AddiVortes: Bayesian Additive Voronoi Tessellations for Machine Learning — AddiVortes-package
text: Key features include: Machine learning regression Bayesian inference Alternative BART using Voronoi tessellations Spatial data analysis modeling Non-parametric regression capabilities Complex function approximation Uncertainty quantification posterior inference

title: AddiVortes: Bayesian Additive Voronoi Tessellations for Machine Learning — AddiVortes-package
text: Stone, . Gosling, J.P. (2025). AddiVortes: (Bayesian) additive Voronoi tessellations. Journal Computational Graphical Statistics.

title: AddiVortes: Bayesian Additive Voronoi Tessellations for Machine Learning — AddiVortes-package
text: Maintainer: John Paul Gosling john-paul.gosling@durham.ac.uk (ORCID) Authors: Adam Stone adam.stone2@durham.ac.uk (ORCID)

title: AddiVortes — AddiVortes
text: AddiVortes function Bayesian nonparametric regression model uses tessellation model relationship covariates output values. model uses backfitting algorithm sample posterior distribution output values tessellation. function returns RMSE value test samples. spherical data, assumed final spherical dimension polar angle: .e. range 0 2*pi.

title: AddiVortes — AddiVortes
code: AddiVortes(   y,   x,   m = 200,   totalMCMCIter = 1200,   mcmcBurnIn = 200,   nu = 6,   q = 0.85,   k = 3,   sd = 0.8,   Omega = min(3, ncol(x)),   LambdaRate = 25,   InitialSigma = "Linear",   thinning = 1,   metric = "E",   catScaling = 1,   showProgress = interactive() )

title: AddiVortes — AddiVortes
text: y vector output values. x matrix data frame covariates. Character factor columns treated categorical variables automatically converted d-1 binary indicator variables via one-hot encoding (first level reference). m number tessellations. totalMCMCIter number iterations. mcmcBurnIn number burn iterations. nu degrees freedom. q quantile. k number centres. sd standard deviation used centre proposals. Omega Omega/(number covariates) prior probability adding dimension. LambdaRate rate Poisson distribution number centres. InitialSigma method used calculate initial variance. thinning thinning rate. metric Either "E" (Euclidean, default) "S" (Spherical). catScaling Numeric scalar controlling scale binary indicator variables created categorical covariates. binary indicator takes values 0 (reference level) catScaling (non-reference level). default value 1 matches range continuous covariates, normalised [-0.5, 0.5] (range = 1) fitting, categorical differences receive comparable weight continuous differences distance calculations. Increase 1 give categorical differences weight; decrease 1 give less weight. Binary indicator columns named <colname>_<level> (e.g. column grp levels "", "B", "C" produces columns grp_B grp_C, "" reference level). showProgress Logical; TRUE, progress bars messages shown fitting.

title: AddiVortes — AddiVortes
text: AddiVortes object containing posterior samples tessellations, dimensions predictions.

title: AddiVortes — AddiVortes
code: # \donttest{ # Simple example with simulated data set.seed(123) x <- matrix(rnorm(50), 10, 5) y <- rnorm(10) # Fit model with reduced iterations for quick example fit <- AddiVortes(y, x, m = 5, totalMCMCIter = 50, mcmcBurnIn = 10)  # Larger example with categorical covariates (d=2 and d=3) and a test set set.seed(456) n_train <- 200 n_test <- 50 x_train <- data.frame(   x1   = rnorm(n_train),   x2   = runif(n_train),   grp2 = sample(c("A", "B"), n_train, replace = TRUE),   grp3 = sample(c("low", "mid", "high"), n_train, replace = TRUE) ) y_train <- x_train$x1 + ifelse(x_train$grp2 == "B", 1, 0) + rnorm(n_train, sd = 0.5)  fit2 <- AddiVortes(y_train, x_train,   m = 10, totalMCMCIter = 200, mcmcBurnIn = 50,   catScaling = 1, showProgress = FALSE )  x_test <- data.frame(   x1   = rnorm(n_test),   x2   = runif(n_test),   grp2 = sample(c("A", "B"), n_test, replace = TRUE),   grp3 = sample(c("low", "mid", "high"), n_test, replace = TRUE) ) y_test <- x_test$x1 + ifelse(x_test$grp2 == "B", 1, 0) + rnorm(n_test, sd = 0.5)  preds <- predict(fit2, x_test, showProgress = FALSE) test_rmse <- sqrt(mean((y_test - preds)^2)) # }

what: Assign Observations to Tessellation Cells — cellIndices
title: Assign Observations to Tessellation Cells — cellIndices
text: given tessellation, function identifies cell (centre) observation belongs based nearest neighbour classification.

title: Assign Observations to Tessellation Cells — cellIndices
code: cellIndices(x, tess, dim, metric = "Euclidean")

title: Assign Observations to Tessellation Cells — cellIndices
text: x numeric matrix covariates row observation. tess numeric matrix representing tessellation centres, row unique centre. dim integer vector specifying column indices x used calculating distance. metric Either "Euclidean" "Spherical".

title: Assign Observations to Tessellation Cells — cellIndices
text: numeric vector integers element corresponds row x value row index nearest centre tess.

title: Assign Observations to Tessellation Cells — cellIndices
text: finds closest tessellation centre observation (row) covariate matrix, considering specified dimensions. achieved using k-nearest neighbour algorithm k=1.

text: constructor AddiVortes class.

title: Create an AddiVortes Object — new_AddiVortes
code: new_AddiVortes(   posteriorTess,   posteriorDim,   posteriorSigma,   posteriorPred,   xCentres,   xRanges,   yCentre,   yRange,   inSampleRmse,   metric = "E",   catEncoding = NULL )

title: Create an AddiVortes Object — new_AddiVortes
text: posteriorTess list posterior samples tessellations. posteriorDim list posterior samples dimensions. posteriorSigma list posterior samples error variance. posteriorPred list posterior samples predictions. xCentres centres covariates. xRanges ranges covariates. yCentre centre output values. yRange range output values. inSampleRmse -sample RMSE. metric metric used scaling covariates (default "E" Euclidean). catEncoding Optional list categorical encoding metadata returned encodeCategories_internal, NULL categorical covariates present.

title: Create an AddiVortes Object — new_AddiVortes
text: object class AddiVortes.

title: Plot Method for AddiVortes — plot.AddiVortes
text: Generates comprehensive diagnostic plots fitted AddiVortes object. function creates multiple diagnostic plots including residuals, MCMC traces sigma, tessellation complexity iterations.

title: Plot Method for AddiVortes — plot.AddiVortes
code: # S3 method for class 'AddiVortes' plot(   x,   x_train,   y_train,   sigma_trace = NULL,   which = c(1, 2, 3),   ask = FALSE,   ... )

title: Plot Method for AddiVortes — plot.AddiVortes
text: x object class AddiVortes, typically result call AddiVortes(). x_train matrix original training covariates. y_train numeric vector original training true outcomes. sigma_trace optional numeric vector sigma values MCMC samples. provided, method attempt extract model object. numeric vector specifying plots generate: 1 = Residuals plot, 2 = Sigma trace, 3 = Tessellation complexity trace, 4 = Predicted vs Observed. Default c(1, 2, 3). ask Logical; TRUE, user asked press Enter plot. ... Additional arguments passed plotting functions.

title: Plot Method for AddiVortes — plot.AddiVortes
text: function called side effect creating plots returns NULL invisibly.

title: Plot Method for AddiVortes — plot.AddiVortes
text: function generates four diagnostic plots: Residuals Plot: Residuals vs fitted values smoothed trend line Sigma Trace: MCMC trace plot error variance parameter Tessellation Complexity: Trace average tessellation size iterations Predicted vs Observed: Scatter plot credible intervals

title: Plot Method for AddiVortes — plot.AddiVortes
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Assuming 'fit' is a trained AddiVortes object plot(fit, x_train = x_train_data, y_train = y_train_data)  # Show only specific plots plot(fit, x_train = x_train_data, y_train = y_train_data, which = c(1, 3))  # With custom sigma trace plot(fit,   x_train = x_train_data, y_train = y_train_data,   sigma_trace = my_sigma_samples ) } # }

title: Predict Method for AddiVortes — predict.AddiVortes
text: Predicts outcomes new data using fitted AddiVortes model object. can return mean predictions, quantiles optionally calculate Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) true outcomes provided.

title: Predict Method for AddiVortes — predict.AddiVortes
code: # S3 method for class 'AddiVortes' predict(   object,   newdata,   type = c("response", "quantile"),   quantiles = c(0.025, 0.975),   interval = c("credible", "prediction"),   showProgress = interactive(),   parallel = TRUE,   cores = NULL,   ... )

title: Predict Method for AddiVortes — predict.AddiVortes
text: object object class AddiVortes, typically result call AddiVortes(). newdata matrix covariates new test set. number columns must match original training data. type type prediction required. default "response" gives mean prediction. alternative "quantile" returns quantiles specified quantiles argument. quantiles numeric vector probabilities compute predictions type = "quantile". interval type interval calculation. default "credible" accounts uncertainty mean (similar lm's confidence interval). alternative "prediction" also includes model's error variance, producing wider intervals (similar lm's prediction interval). showProgress Logical; TRUE, progress bar shown prediction. parallel Logical; TRUE (default), predictions computed parallel. cores number CPU cores use parallel processing. NULL (default), defaults one less total number available cores. ... arguments passed methods (currently unused).

title: Predict Method for AddiVortes — predict.AddiVortes
text: type = "response", numeric vector mean predictions. type = "quantile", matrix row corresponds observation newdata column quantile.

title: Predict Method for AddiVortes — predict.AddiVortes
text: function relies internal helper function applyScaling_internal available environment, used main AddiVortes function. interval = "prediction" type = "quantile", function samples additional Gaussian noise variance equal sampled sigma squared posterior. accounts inherent variability individual predictions, just uncertainty mean function. noise added scaled space unscaling predictions.

title: Predict Method for AddiVortes — predict.AddiVortes
code: # \donttest{ # Fit a model set.seed(123) X <- matrix(rnorm(100), 20, 5) Y <- rnorm(20) fit <- AddiVortes(Y, X, m = 5, totalMCMCIter = 50, mcmcBurnIn = 10)  # New data for prediction X_new <- matrix(rnorm(25), 5, 5)  # Mean predictions pred_mean <- predict(fit, X_new, type = "response")  # Credible intervals (uncertainty in mean only) pred_conf <- predict(fit, X_new,   type = "quantile",   interval = "credible",   quantiles = c(0.025, 0.975) )  # Prediction intervals (includes error variance) pred_pred <- predict(fit, X_new,   type = "quantile",   interval = "prediction",   quantiles = c(0.025, 0.975) )  # Prediction intervals are wider than credible intervals mean(pred_pred[, 2] - pred_pred[, 1]) > mean(pred_conf[, 2] - pred_conf[, 1]) #> [1] TRUE # }
